mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers photosynthesis as initiation of energy conversion (A), including biological importance (A.1), factors influencing rate and efficiency (A.2), mechanism determination (A.3), chloroplast as site (A.4), photosynthetic pigments (A.5), energetics of photoelectron transfer (A.6), and the carbon reduction cycle (A.7).

Metabolism as the totality of chemical processes (B), divided into catabolism for fragmentation of complex molecules (B.1), cellular respiration and oxidation for energy conservation (B.2), anabolism for biosynthesis of cell components (B.3), and metabolic regulation (B.4).
Redox reactions involving electron transfer are central to cellular respiration and oxidative phosphorylation (B.2), the combustion of food materials and conservation of energy.
Biochemistry underpins both photosynthesis (A) and metabolism (B), characterizing macromolecules and enzymatic processes central to bioenergetics and biosynthesis.
Energy conservation through photoelectron transfer and photophosphorylation (A.6), and combustion of food materials with conservation of energy in cellular respiration (B.2).
Ecosystems depend on primary production through photosynthesis as the initiation of energy conversion in the biosphere (A.1), with energy flows and nutrient cycles.
Nutrition supplies nutrients for catabolism of complex food molecules (B.1) and provides building blocks for anabolic biosynthesis of cell components (B.3).
Carbon participates in the metabolic path of carbon in photosynthesis through the carbon reduction cycle (A.7), linking CO2 fixation to organic compound production.
CO2 is the primary carbon source fixed into organic compounds through the Calvin cycle in the carbon reduction cycle of photosynthesis (A.7).
Oxygen is released by oxygenic photosynthesis splitting water (A) and is essential for aerobic cellular respiration (B.2).
Iron in heme groups enables oxygen transport and iron-containing redox enzymes drive cellular respiration (B.2), central to oxidative transduction.
The chloroplast as the site of photosynthesis (A.4) and cellular metabolism encompassing all chemical processes (B) occur at the cellular level.
Plants are the primary photosynthetic organisms (A), with chloroplasts in green plant cells as the site of the photosynthetic process (A.4).
Cyanobacteria perform oxygenic photosynthesis (A), and bacteria exhibit diverse metabolisms relevant to chemosynthetic energy production.
Breathing provides oxygen for cellular respiration and removes carbon dioxide (B.2), supporting the combustion of food materials.
Digestion is the catabolic breakdown of complex food macromolecules into absorbable monomers (B.1), the first step in fragmentation of complex molecules.
Carbohydrates are primary products of the carbon reduction cycle in photosynthesis (A.7), substrates for catabolic energy release (B.1), and components synthesized in anabolism (B.3).
Hormones regulate metabolism through negative feedback homeostasis (B.4), controlling enzyme activity and metabolic pathway flux.
Lipids undergo beta-oxidation in catabolism (B.1) and are synthesized in anabolic pathways (B.3), central to energy storage and membrane formation.
Enzyme catalysis accelerates metabolic reactions, with allosteric regulation governing reaction rates relevant to metabolic regulation (B.4).
Thermodynamic principles govern energy conservation in photophosphorylation (A.6) and cellular respiration (B.2), with entropy and free energy determining reaction feasibility.
Water is split in oxygenic photosynthesis releasing O2 (A), functioning as the universal solvent in all metabolic reactions.
Algae are photosynthetic organisms performing photosynthesis (A), functioning as principal primary producers in aquatic ecosystems.
The origin of life involved evolution of prebiotic metabolic pathways (B), including self-replicating molecular systems and early energy metabolism.
Eukaryotes contain chloroplasts for photosynthesis (A.4) and mitochondria for cellular respiration (B.2), both acquired through endosymbiogenesis.
Food provides complex molecules for catabolic fragmentation (B.1) and combustion in cellular respiration (B.2), with photosynthesis as the primary energy source.
Chemical reactions transform reactants into products through bond-breaking and bond-forming events, fundamental to all metabolic processes (B), with enzymes catalyzing biochemical reactions.
Photosynthesis as the biological importance of energy conversion (A.1) and metabolism as the totality of chemical processes sustaining life (B) are fundamental properties of all living systems.
